title: Apple
content: Apple is a popular fruit, available in many varieties (red, green, yellow). Key nutrients: fiber (especially pectin), vitamin C, and antioxidants like quercetin, concentrated mostly in the skin. Health benefits: supports heart health and healthy cholesterol levels, aids digestion, and helps regulate blood sugar due to its fiber content. Best eaten with the skin on for maximum fiber and antioxidants.
keywords: Apple, fruit nutrition, pectin, quercetin

title: Banana
content: Banana is a widely eaten tropical fruit. Key nutrients: potassium, vitamin B6, vitamin C, and fiber. Health benefits: supports heart and muscle function through potassium, provides quick energy from natural sugars, and aids digestion. A convenient, easily digestible pre- or post-exercise snack.
keywords: Banana, potassium, fruit nutrition

title: Orange
content: Orange is a citrus fruit known for its high vitamin C content. Key nutrients: vitamin C, fiber, folate, and antioxidants (flavonoids). Health benefits: strengthens immune function, supports collagen production and skin health, and improves iron absorption from plant foods when eaten together.
keywords: Orange, citrus, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Mango
content: Mango is a sweet tropical fruit often called the 'king of fruits'. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, folate, and fiber. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A and C, and aids digestion; best enjoyed in moderation due to its natural sugar content, especially for those managing blood sugar.
keywords: Mango, tropical fruit, vitamin a, fruit nutrition

title: Kiwi
content: Kiwi (kiwifruit) is a small fruit with green or gold flesh and edible black seeds. Key nutrients: exceptionally high in vitamin C (more per serving than an orange), vitamin K, fiber, and potassium. Health benefits: strong immune support, aids digestion due to fiber and the enzyme actinidin, and supports healthy blood clotting via vitamin K.
keywords: Kiwi, kiwifruit, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Grapes
content: Grapes are small, sweet fruits that grow in clusters, available in green, red, and black varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and antioxidants like resveratrol (concentrated in the skin, especially red/black grapes). Health benefits: antioxidants support heart health and may help reduce inflammation; best eaten in moderation due to natural sugar content.
keywords: Grapes, resveratrol, fruit nutrition

title: Papaya
content: Papaya is a tropical fruit with orange flesh and black seeds. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, folate, and the digestive enzyme papain. Health benefits: aids digestion (papain helps break down protein), supports immune function and eye health, and has anti-inflammatory properties.
keywords: Papaya, papain, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Pineapple
content: Pineapple is a tropical fruit known for its sweet-tart flavor. Key nutrients: vitamin C, manganese, and the enzyme bromelain. Health benefits: bromelain has anti-inflammatory properties and aids protein digestion; vitamin C supports immune function and wound healing.
keywords: Pineapple, bromelain, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Guava
content: Guava is a tropical fruit with edible skin and seeds. Key nutrients: extremely high in vitamin C (often more than citrus fruits), fiber, and lycopene (in pink-fleshed varieties). Health benefits: strong immune support, aids digestion due to high fiber content, and supports heart health through lycopene and potassium.
keywords: Guava, vitamin c, tropical fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Pomegranate
content: Pomegranate is a fruit with a thick skin containing many juicy, seeded arils. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and powerful antioxidants called punicalagins. Health benefits: supports heart health, has strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, and may support joint health.
keywords: Pomegranate, antioxidants, fruit nutrition

title: Watermelon
content: Watermelon is a large, water-rich fruit (about 92% water). Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, and the antioxidant lycopene. Health benefits: excellent for hydration, supports heart health and skin health through lycopene and vitamin C, and is naturally low in calories.
keywords: Watermelon, hydration, lycopene, fruit nutrition

title: Strawberry
content: Strawberry is a sweet, red berry. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C, manganese, folate, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports immune function and skin health, and antioxidants may help reduce inflammation and support heart health.
keywords: Strawberry, berries, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Blueberry
content: Blueberry is a small, dark blue berry known for its high antioxidant content. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and anthocyanins (the antioxidant giving them their color). Health benefits: supports brain health and memory, may reduce inflammation, and supports heart health.
keywords: Blueberry, berries, anthocyanins, fruit nutrition

title: Raspberry
content: Raspberry is a small, red or black aggregate fruit. Key nutrients: high in fiber, vitamin C, manganese, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health due to high fiber content, immune support from vitamin C, and antioxidants that may help reduce inflammation.
keywords: Raspberry, berries, fiber, fruit nutrition

title: Muskmelon (Cantaloupe)
content: Muskmelon (cantaloupe) is a sweet, orange-fleshed melon. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, and potassium. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A and C, and is hydrating with a high water content.
keywords: Muskmelon, cantaloupe, melon, fruit nutrition

title: Pear
content: Pear is a sweet fruit with a grainy texture, eaten with or without the skin. Key nutrients: fiber (especially in the skin), vitamin C, and vitamin K. Health benefits: supports digestive health and healthy cholesterol through its high fiber content, and provides steady energy from natural sugars.
keywords: Pear, fiber, fruit nutrition

title: Plum
content: Plum is a stone fruit with smooth skin, available in many varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health (dried plums/prunes are well known for this), supports bone health via vitamin K, and antioxidants support overall cell health.
keywords: Plum, prune, stone fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Peach
content: Peach is a sweet, fuzzy-skinned stone fruit. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin A, and fiber. Health benefits: supports skin and immune health through vitamin C, and aids digestion through its fiber content.
keywords: Peach, stone fruit, fruit nutrition

title: Apricot
content: Apricot is a small, orange stone fruit, eaten fresh or dried. Key nutrients: vitamin A, vitamin C, potassium, and fiber. Health benefits: supports eye health through its high vitamin A (beta-carotene) content, and supports heart health and digestion.
keywords: Apricot, stone fruit, beta-carotene, fruit nutrition

title: Fig
content: Fig is a soft, sweet fruit with a fiber-rich, seeded interior, eaten fresh or dried. Key nutrients: fiber, calcium, potassium, and antioxidants. Health benefits: supports digestive health and, notably, provides more calcium than many other fruits, supporting bone health.
keywords: Fig, calcium, fiber, fruit nutrition

title: Lemon and Lime
content: Lemon and lime are tart citrus fruits, usually used for their juice and zest rather than eaten whole. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C, along with citric acid and flavonoid antioxidants. Health benefits: supports immune function, improves iron absorption from plant foods when added to meals, and adds flavor to water/dishes without added sugar.
keywords: Lemon, lime, citrus, vitamin c, fruit nutrition

title: Avocado
content: Avocado is a nutrient-dense fruit known for its creamy texture and healthy fat content. Key nutrients: monounsaturated (heart-healthy) fats, fiber, potassium, vitamin E, and folate. Health benefits: supports heart health through healthy fats, aids nutrient absorption of fat-soluble vitamins from other foods, and supports satiety due to its fat and fiber content.
keywords: Avocado, healthy fats, monounsaturated fat, fruit nutrition

title: Coconut
content: Coconut is a fruit whose flesh, water, and oil are all commonly consumed. Key nutrients: coconut flesh is high in fiber and medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs); coconut water is rich in electrolytes like potassium. Health benefits: coconut water supports hydration and electrolyte balance; coconut flesh provides fiber, though it is also high in saturated fat, so moderation is advised.
keywords: Coconut, coconut water, MCT, fruit nutrition

title: Spinach
content: Spinach is a leafy green vegetable. Key nutrients: iron, vitamin K, vitamin A, folate, and antioxidants (lutein). Health benefits: supports healthy blood (iron and folate for red blood cell production), bone health via vitamin K, and eye health via lutein and vitamin A. Pairing with vitamin C rich foods improves iron absorption.
keywords: Spinach, leafy greens, iron, vegetable nutrition

title: Broccoli
content: Broccoli is a cruciferous vegetable with edible flower buds and stalk. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and sulforaphane (a compound linked to cancer-protective effects). Health benefits: strong immune and bone support, aids digestion, and its sulforaphane content is associated with reduced cancer risk in research.
keywords: Broccoli, cruciferous vegetable, sulforaphane, vegetable nutrition

title: Carrot
content: Carrot is a root vegetable, typically orange, though also found in purple, yellow, and white varieties. Key nutrients: very high in beta-carotene (converted to vitamin A), fiber, and vitamin K. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A, and aids digestion through fiber.
keywords: Carrot, beta-carotene, root vegetable, vegetable nutrition

title: Tomato
content: Tomato is botanically a fruit but commonly used as a vegetable. Key nutrients: vitamin C, potassium, folate, and lycopene (an antioxidant more available when tomatoes are cooked). Health benefits: supports heart health through lycopene and potassium, and immune function through vitamin C.
keywords: Tomato, lycopene, vegetable nutrition

title: Potato
content: Potato is a starchy root vegetable and a staple food in many cuisines. Key nutrients: vitamin C, potassium (more than a banana), vitamin B6, and fiber (especially in the skin). Health benefits: provides energy through carbohydrates, supports heart function via potassium; preparation method (baked/boiled vs. fried) significantly affects its overall healthfulness.
keywords: Potato, starchy vegetable, potassium, vegetable nutrition

title: Onion
content: Onion is a pungent bulb vegetable used widely in cooking. Key nutrients: vitamin C, fiber, and antioxidants (quercetin) along with prebiotic fibers. Health benefits: quercetin has anti-inflammatory properties, and its prebiotic fiber supports healthy gut bacteria.
keywords: Onion, quercetin, prebiotic, vegetable nutrition

title: Cucumber
content: Cucumber is a high-water-content vegetable, often eaten raw. Key nutrients: mostly water (about 95%), with some vitamin K and potassium. Health benefits: excellent for hydration, low in calories, and its fiber (especially the skin) supports digestion.
keywords: Cucumber, hydration, low calorie, vegetable nutrition

title: Cauliflower
content: Cauliflower is a cruciferous vegetable, related to broccoli and cabbage. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, fiber, and choline. Health benefits: supports immune and bone health, aids digestion, and is a popular low-carbohydrate substitute for grains like rice in many diets.
keywords: Cauliflower, cruciferous vegetable, low carb, vegetable nutrition

title: Cabbage
content: Cabbage is a leafy cruciferous vegetable, available in green, red/purple, and savoy varieties. Key nutrients: vitamin C, vitamin K, and fiber. Health benefits: supports immune and bone health, aids digestion, and fermented cabbage (like sauerkraut/kimchi) also provides probiotics for gut health.
keywords: Cabbage, cruciferous vegetable, sauerkraut, vegetable nutrition

title: Bell Pepper (Capsicum)
content: Bell pepper (capsicum) is a sweet pepper available in green, red, yellow, and orange varieties. Key nutrients: very high in vitamin C (red peppers have more than oranges), vitamin A, and antioxidants. Health benefits: strong immune support, supports eye health, and adds color/nutrients to meals with few calories.
keywords: Bell pepper, capsicum, vitamin c, vegetable nutrition

title: Sweet Potato
content: Sweet potato is a starchy root vegetable with orange, purple, or white flesh. Key nutrients: very high in beta-carotene (vitamin A), fiber, and potassium. Health benefits: supports eye health and immune function through vitamin A, aids digestion via fiber, and has a lower glycemic impact than regular potatoes when boiled.
keywords: Sweet potato, beta-carotene, root vegetable, vegetable nutrition

title: Beetroot
content: Beetroot is a deep red-purple root vegetable. Key nutrients: folate, potassium, fiber, and nitrates (natural compounds that convert to nitric oxide in the body). Health benefits: nitrates may support healthy blood pressure and exercise performance, and folate supports red blood cell production.
keywords: Beetroot, beet, nitrates, vegetable nutrition
